rules.title: Ordinals Behaving Like Adjectives - Overview
rules.text: Ordinal numbers (first, second, third, etc.) function as adjectives when they modify nouns by indicating position, rank, or order in a sequence. Like other adjectives, ordinals come before the noun they modify and agree with it in number. They answer the question 'which one?' by specifying order.
examples: The first impression is often the most lasting.
She won first prize in the competition.
They live on the third floor.
This is my fifth attempt.

rules.title: First - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'First' is the most common ordinal adjective, indicating the initial position in a sequence. It can modify singular or plural nouns and often implies importance or priority beyond mere position.

rows: The first impression is often the most lasting.
She won first prize in the competition.
This is my first attempt at baking bread.
The first three students will receive awards.
First place goes to the winner.

examples: The first day of school is always exciting.
He made a first impression that lasted.
First base is closest to home plate.
She's in her first year of college.

rules.title: Second - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'Second' indicates the position immediately following the first. It's commonly used in comparisons, rankings, and when presenting alternatives or options.

rows: This is my second cup of coffee today.
The second option seems more practical.
She lives on the second floor.
The second chapter is more interesting.

examples: Second thoughts made her reconsider.
He's my second cousin.
The second half of the game was better.
This is our second meeting this month.

rules.title: Third - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'Third' indicates the position after the second. It's frequently used for rankings, floors, repeated occurrences, and sequential items.

rows: They live on the third floor.
This is the third time I've explained this.
He came in third in the race.
Third base is near the dugout.

examples: The third chapter introduces the villain.
She's in her third trimester.
Third gear is for higher speeds.
The third option is the cheapest.

rules.title: Fourth - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'Fourth' indicates the position after the third. Commonly used for academic years, quarters, sequential events, and rankings.

rows: July is the fourth month with 31 days.
This is our fourth meeting this week.
She's in her fourth year of medical school.
Fourth place still gets a ribbon.

examples: The fourth floor has the best view.
This is my fourth revision.
Fourth gear is the smoothest.
The Fourth of July is a holiday.

rules.title: Fifth - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'Fifth' indicates the position after the fourth. Used for numbered works, revisions, counting items, and sequential positions.

rows: This is the fifth draft of my essay.
He drank his fifth glass of water.
Fifth Avenue is famous for shopping.

examples: The fifth element is surprise.
She's my fifth caller today.
Fifth place earns a certificate.
The fifth inning was scoreless.

rules.title: Sixth through Tenth - Ordinals as Adjectives
rules.text: Higher ordinals (sixth, seventh, eighth, ninth, tenth) follow the same adjectival pattern, modifying nouns to indicate their position in longer sequences.

rows: The seventh day is for rest.
The eighth grade is middle school.
The ninth inning decided the game.
She lives on the sixth floor.
The eighth chapter is the climax.

examples: The sixth month is June.
Seventh heaven means perfect happiness.
The eighth wonder of the world.
Ninth place is still respectable.
The tenth commandment warns against coveting.

rules.title: Last - Ordinal as Adjective
rules.text: 'Last' is a special ordinal indicating the final position in a sequence. It implies finality and often suggests importance, urgency, or no future opportunities.

rows: That was the last piece of cake.
She had the last word in the argument.
This is my last chance to apply.
The last train leaves at midnight.
Last place is still an achievement.

examples: The last day of school is exciting.
He spent his last dollar.
Last call for boarding!
The last chapter ties everything together.

rules.title: Ordinals with Plural Nouns
rules.text: Ordinals can modify plural nouns when referring to groups or ranges. The ordinal remains singular while the noun is plural.
examples: The first two winners receive prizes.
The last three items are on sale.
The next several weeks will be busy.
The first few years were difficult.

rules.title: Ordinals in Compound Modifiers
rules.text: Ordinals can combine with other adjectives or nouns to create compound modifiers, often hyphenated when preceding the noun.
examples: First-degree burns are minor.
Second-generation immigrants face unique challenges.
Third-world countries need support.
Last-ditch effort to save the project.

rules.title: Ordinals in Idiomatic Expressions
rules.text: Many ordinals appear in fixed expressions and idioms where they function as adjectives but carry special meanings beyond simple position.

rows: Driving became second nature to her.
I felt like a third wheel on their date.
He's a fifth wheel on this project.
That insult was the last straw.
She had second thoughts about quitting.

examples: At first glance, it seemed easy.
Second opinions are valuable in medicine.
The third time's the charm.
Last but not least, thank you all.

rules.title: Ordinals vs Cardinals - Grammatical Comparison
rules.text: Understanding when numbers function as ordinals (adjectives showing position) versus cardinals (showing quantity) is essential for proper usage.
examples: Cardinal: I read ten pages. / Ordinal: I stopped at the tenth page.
Cardinal: She has four children. / Ordinal: Her fourth child was born yesterday.
Cardinal: We need six volunteers. / Ordinal: The sixth volunteer gets a prize.

rules.title: Position of Ordinal Adjectives
rules.text: Ordinals typically appear before the noun they modify, following the standard English adjective word order. When multiple adjectives are present, ordinals usually come first.
examples: The first big mistake was costly.
Her second beautiful painting sold quickly.
Our third annual meeting is next week.
That last difficult question stumped everyone.

rules.title: Summary - Ordinals as Adjectives
rules.text: Quick reference guide for understanding ordinal numbers functioning as adjectives in English grammar.

rows: first man, first woman, first children
